rank_rationale: #1: clearest flap body map for 4-6m shared touch-and-name.
include_rationale: Body words land best when you touch the real tummy while the flap lifts. Keep it a giggle game on the changing mat, not a quiz. One or two flaps is enough; stop while smiles are still easy and put the book aside.

Song plus touch makes knees and toes stick without sounding like a lesson. Sing once, touch once, then stop while they still smile. Keep the book as a prompt on the mat rather than a full workout through every verse.

product_name: Ten Little Fingers and Ten Little Toes
rank_rationale: #3: hand-and-foot focus matches this age’s discovery.
include_rationale: Fingers and toes are the body parts they can finally see and grab. Count aloud while they hold a foot; the book is the refrain. Keep one short count, then stop while the shared look still feels friendly on the sofa.

Body awareness grows when movement joins the name. Do one animal action on the mat, then close the book before it becomes a workout. Stay close, keep it playful, and stop while smiles are still easy after that single action.

product_name: The Nose, Toes and Tummy Book
rank_rationale: #5: interactive body play with UK stock.
include_rationale: Playful prompts beat dry labels. Beep a nose, then kiss a tummy; the page is permission to be silly together during nose toes play on the mat. Keep one prompt per sitting and stop while giggles are still easy rather than racing every page.

rank_rationale: #6: face-first body entry; Ladybird capped later.
include_rationale: Faces are still the strongest body cue. Point to eyes and mouth on the page, then on your own face, then stop. Keep turns short on the sofa and put the book away while the shared look still feels friendly.

rank_rationale: #7: second Ladybird; brand capped at two.
include_rationale: Animals give a second route into body words when face books feel finished. Name one part per page, then close the covers. Stay close on the sofa and stop while the shared look still feels friendly rather than finishing every animal shape tonight.

rank_rationale: #8: touchy-feely body vocabulary via animal parts.
include_rationale: Texture plus naming keeps hands busy while mouths want everything on the puppy body pages. One patch per sitting is enough. Close the book while the shared look still feels friendly and save the rest of the patches for another awake spell.

If Puppy is sticky with milk, Cat keeps the same refrain with new textured patches on the body pages. Cap Usborne at two on this card so the shelf does not fill with the same pattern. Stop while the shared look still feels friendly.

product_name: Brown Bear, Brown Bear, What Do You See?
rank_rationale: #10: naming practice via animals; body-first titles ranked higher.
include_rationale: Not a pure body book, but pointing at animal faces and feet still trains the look and name habit with a colour animal chorus. Use when dedicated body titles are all sticky. Keep one animal, then stop while the shared look still feels friendly.
